findings.title: Main + 2-4 Burner Account Structure to Grow X Presence Fast
findings.claim: Running one premium 'main' account supported by 2–4 high-volume burner accounts is the core engine for rapid X follower growth and OF revenue.
findings.what_they_do: One main account is treated as the premium asset — high quality content only, no full nudity, goal of 50–100K impressions per post. Two to four burner accounts run parallel at higher volume (3–5 posts/day vs 1–3 on main), used for trend testing, engagement farming, and follower accumulation. Once burners hit 1–5K followers, all traffic is funneled to the main via pinned tweets, bio links, and reply CTAs. Main account holds the OF link; burners never monetize directly.
findings.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is a documented GAP (§5). Palm has no X/Twitter presence or strategy documented. This is a fully absent channel for Palm.
findings.recommendation: Build a 1-main + 2-burner X account structure for each real creator. Assign a VA to run burner engagement (spam commenting, trend monitoring, retweet loop) daily. Main account managed by editor/ops. Start with one creator as a test before rolling out.

sources.quote: You're going to have two to four accounts in this depending on where you're at. The account one is going to be your main account.

findings.title: Pay X Promo Pages $50–$150 per Post to Inject 500–2K Followers Fast
findings.claim: Third-party X 'promotional pages' (50K–500K followers) that post teaser/clickbait content about a creator and then get a staged reply from the creator's account are a highly cost-effective paid acquisition channel on X.
findings.what_they_do: Find accounts (50K+ followers) that post blurry/frame teaser content with clickbait captions like 'Holy shit I can't believe she did this on camera — thread.' Pay them $50–$150 per post. The creator's burner or main account then 'discovers' the post and replies with the real content plus an OF CTA (e.g., 'OMG that's me — I made it free on my OF for the next 17 people'). Identifies promo pages by: always mention 'thread' in caption, post multiple different models, 50K+ followers. Frequency: run a promo every 2–4 weeks per account that is stagnant. One promo post can yield 500–2,000 new followers per day.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no X strategy at all (§5 GAP). Palm does use paid promotion for IG (implied in pipeline), but no equivalent X promo-page tactic exists. This is a new channel + tactic combination.
findings.recommendation: Source 3–5 verified X promo pages in each creator's niche. Negotiate a test post at $50–$100. Pre-script the creator's 'reply' post with OF CTA and urgency hook. Track follower gain and OF tracking link conversions per promo. Build a promo-page vendor list in Airtable (name, follower count, niche, rate, past results).

sources.quote: These are accounts with absolutely massive following, 50 to 500,000 followers that post teaser content for your models.
I want you to DM them saying, hey, I'm looking to promote my account. What are your rates?

findings.title: Trend-Jack Active X Formats Daily for Guaranteed High-Impression Posts
findings.claim: Hopping on a trending X format with high-quality content almost guarantees 50K–200K+ impressions per post, far outperforming Instagram trend participation.
findings.what_they_do: Monitor X daily for trending formats in the creator's niche. As soon as a format gains traction (e.g., 'Hey Grok, put me in a bikini' AI image trend in Jan 2026), immediately produce a post in that format with high-quality creative and post it. Don't wait for certainty — first 5,000–10,000 participants get disproportionate reach. Historical trend formats used: POV/relatable scenery, 'Which one would you pick' poll + photos, 'Rate my outfit 1–10' mirror pic, AI-generated content prompts, 'This or that' polls. Cadence: check competitor/big accounts daily; if a format is getting traction, copy it fast.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is already data-driven and scrapes viral Reels from IG inspo accounts — this is exactly the same logic applied to X. Palm's system could theoretically be extended to scrape X trending formats. Currently Palm has no X monitoring in the Apify/inspo pipeline.
findings.recommendation: Add X trend monitoring to the content pipeline: include 5–10 X creator accounts in the Apify scrape or add a manual daily X check as a VA task. When a trending format is identified, create an X-specific task card in Airtable so the creator can produce the asset same-day. Mirror Palm's existing inspo-promote-film loop for X.

sources.quote: Trendjacking. This is the cheat code. On Twitter, trends are everything. If you hop on a trend correctly and at the right time, you're guaranteed to blow up.
If you're one of the first 5,000 people, 10,000 people to hop on a trend and do it correctly with high quality, guaranteed to blow up.

findings.topic: X content rules — tease vs. reveal
findings.title: No Full Nudity on X — Use Tease-Only Content to Pull Higher-Spending Fans
findings.claim: Posting full nudity on X attracts low-spending free-content seekers; tease-only premium content attracts higher-demographic buyers who will pay on OF.
findings.what_they_do: Main account posts only professional photos, good angles, clean aesthetic — no full nudity. Full explicit content is reserved exclusively for the OF vault. The X page functions purely as top-of-funnel tease. No OF link on burner accounts — only on the main. Strategy is: build personality + tease body, never reveal. Explicit quote: 'Do tease, but don't reveal. People follow people, not just bodies.'
findings.vs_us: Palm's OF vault content is a documented GAP (§2 — thin). This finding reinforces a content tiering principle: social = tease, OF = explicit. Palm's social pipeline (§1) produces top-of-funnel content but has no explicit rule documented about nudity levels per platform. This is a rule Palm should codify.
findings.recommendation: Add a content-tier rule to Palm's SOP: X/IG/TikTok = tease only (no nudity, professional quality). OF vault = exclusive explicit. Document this as a creator briefing item in onboarding and in the editor task spec so editors never post full nudity to social channels.

sources.quote: Do not post full nudity. Save that for OF. Do tease, but don't reveal.
If you post childish content or you post full nude content, you will pull lower spending fans.

findings.topic: X engagement farming via competitor reply hijacking
findings.title: Reply-Hijack Competitor X Posts With Relevant Matching Content to Steal Followers
findings.claim: Replying to competing creators' posts with a relevant matching photo of your model hijacks their engagement and drives profile visits and follows at zero cost.
findings.what_they_do: Identify 10–20 OF creator accounts in the same niche on X. When those accounts post (e.g., bikini beach pic), the burner account replies with a relevant matching photo of the managed creator and a natural caption (e.g., 'What about my bikini at the beach?'). Also retweet competitor posts to appear in front of their audience. Comment on any post going viral in the broader niche to farm free impressions. VA hired to execute this spam engagement loop throughout the day.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no X presence (§5 GAP). Palm does no competitor engagement farming on any platform currently. The VA execution model aligns with Palm's contractor model (Amin for posting) — this would be a new VA task scope.
findings.recommendation: When standing up X for a creator, hire or assign a VA specifically for X engagement tasks: daily competitor reply loop, retweet rotation, viral-post comment farming. Define a task card type in Airtable for 'X daily engagement' with a checklist. Budget ~1–2 hrs/day per creator for this role.

sources.quote: Reply to that with relevant things. What about my bikini at the beach? And then attach a photo of you at your model at a beach. What this is going to do is hijack their engagement.
You guys can hire a VA to do this. I have my VAs to do this. Just make sure your spam engagement.

findings.title: Boost Organically-Proven X Posts for $20–$50 to Amplify Already-Working Content
findings.claim: Paying $20–$50 to boost a tweet that already hit 10K+ impressions organically compounds reach cheaply because the algorithm signals are already proven.
findings.what_they_do: Let content run organically first. If a post reaches 10K impressions without paid spend, that is the signal to boost it via Twitter's native promotion tool. Spend $20–$50 per boost. Logic: high organic engagement + paid boost = outsized reach because Twitter's algorithm already validated the content. Only boost proven winners, never cold content.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no X paid strategy (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG promotions for creators but no equivalent X paid amplification. The 'prove it organically first, then pay to amplify' logic is a sound principle Palm could also apply to IG Reels.
findings.recommendation: Add a paid-boost rule to the X playbook: any post crossing 10K impressions organically gets a $20–$50 boost within 24 hours. Track boosted-post ROI via tracking links (Palm already uses tracking links per §0 context in video). Apply the same 'organic-first, then amplify' logic to IG content where Palm already promotes.

sources.quote: If a post hit 10K impressions organically, promote it. Twitter will let you boost tweets for 20 to 50 bucks, and this puts your content in front of people for more reach.

findings.topic: Traffic source tracking links per platform
findings.title: Use Per-Platform Tracking Links to Attribute OF Revenue to Traffic Sources
findings.claim: Using a dedicated tracking link for each traffic source (e.g., one link for Twitter) lets you measure exactly how much OF revenue each platform generates.
findings.what_they_do: Each traffic channel (X account, IG, promo page, etc.) gets its own tracking/inflow link. Griffin shows a single Twitter tracking link that generated $186K in OF revenue, proving channel-level attribution. This enables decisions like 'Twitter is responsible for ~40–50% of this creator's revenue growth' rather than guessing.
findings.vs_us: Palm uses tracking links (mentioned in the video's own intro showing Palm-style data). However, Palm's traffic strategy documentation is a GAP (§5) — it's unclear whether Palm systematically assigns per-platform tracking links for all creators and all channels, or whether this is ad hoc.
findings.recommendation: Standardize per-platform OF tracking links as a mandatory step in creator setup. For every active traffic channel (IG bio, X main account, Reddit, promo pages), create a unique tracking link at onboarding. Store these in the Creators table in Airtable with a 'Traffic Source Links' field. Pull revenue-by-source into the whale-hunting / fan intelligence dashboard (§9) for channel-level ROI reporting.

sources.quote: This is a tracking link in inflow from one of our Twitter accounts on our creator using this strategy and we've generated $186,000 through this link.

findings.title: Source chatters from onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, OFM Jobs — never Telegram
findings.claim: The three reliable hiring platforms for chatters are onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs; Telegram chatters are consistently low quality.
findings.what_they_do: Post job listings on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Explicitly avoid sourcing from Telegram groups. Also mentions some Facebook groups and other Filipino hiring sites as secondary options.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team (§8); no internal hiring process exists yet. When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr out), it has no documented sourcing channels. This is a direct gap to fill.
findings.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, establish onlinejobs.ph as the primary sourcing channel. Create a blacklist policy against Telegram-sourced chatters. Document this as the first step in the future chatter hiring SOP.

sources.quote: post a job post on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Those are like the three main hiring sites. Don't get them off of Telegram.

findings.title: Multi-stage application filter: attention check + AP score + template effort scoring
findings.claim: Embedding a small compliance instruction in the job post (e.g. 'write I understand at the bottom') combined with checking onlinejobs.ph Application Points and requiring a filled template eliminates ~40–50% of low-quality applicants before any interview.
findings.what_they_do: Three-layer pre-screen: (1) embed a hidden instruction in the job post requiring a specific phrase to prove they read it; (2) on onlinejobs.ph, prioritize applicants who spent more Application Points (higher AP = higher motivation, lower AP = mass-applying); (3) send all applicants a structured template (name, age, experience, English level, open-ended questions like 'describe your communication style') and filter out anyone who answers lazily or skips fields.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring baseline (§8 outsourced). This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
findings.recommendation: Build these three filters into Palm's future chatter hiring SOP as Stage 1. The AP-score filter is onlinejobs.ph-specific and low-effort; include it as a default screen. Design the template with at least one open-ended question specifically to measure effort, not content.

sources.quote: put a simple instruction next to the job post. So, if you apply, text me I understand at the bottom of your application
you always want to look at the AP... if there's an application with just one AP, one application point, means he probably sent it to like a thousand other agencies
they should fill out a template sent in the back to you with like the name, the age, job experience, English level, and just a couple of questions

findings.title: Group-chat situational awareness test to screen for sales instinct before any live interview
findings.claim: A 10-question situational/emotional intelligence test sent to all shortlisted applicants in a single group chat efficiently identifies candidates with natural sales instinct without requiring individual calls.
findings.what_they_do: Create a group chat (WhatsApp or Telegram) with all applicants who passed Stage 1. Send a Google Docs with 10 situational questions designed to reveal emotional intelligence and objection-handling instinct — not chatting skill. Example: 'You own a vegan restaurant and someone asks for steak — what do you say?' The 'right' answer reframes the objection and redirects to an alternative rather than refusing. This filters out poor communicators before investing time in live interviews.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter screening baseline (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward for the in-house chat team. Palm's current external chat team presumably handles their own quality — Palm has no visibility into how those chatters were vetted.
findings.recommendation: Develop a Palm-specific situational awareness test (10 questions) as Stage 2 of the future chatter hiring pipeline. Frame questions around sales redirection rather than OF-specific knowledge. Use a group chat delivery mechanism to batch-process applicants efficiently.

sources.quote: create a group chat with all of the applicants and you're going to give them their first test... a situational awareness test
you are the owner of a vegan restaurant and someone comes in asking for real meat, for a steak. What would you tell that person?

findings.title: Discord live call with screen-share: WPM test + English test + physical setup camera reveal
findings.claim: A live Discord call with screen-sharing — covering typing speed, English proficiency, and a live camera reveal of the candidate's physical workspace — surfaces reliability signals that asynchronous tests cannot.
findings.what_they_do: Stage 3 live assessment via Discord with screen-share: (1) words-per-minute typing test (candidates will cheat; if you can't catch it, they earned it); (2) live English proficiency test using any free online tool; (3) ask the candidate to turn their camera and film their desk/setup in real time. The desk reveal is flagged as unique and highly valuable — an organized vs. disorganized desk is treated as a proxy for how they'll manage their work. Philosophy: 'how you do one thing is how you do everything.'
findings.vs_us: No internal live vetting process (§8 outsourced). The desk/setup reveal is a novel, zero-cost signal not commonly used. Build-toward for in-house chat hiring.
findings.recommendation: Include the three-part live Discord assessment as Stage 3 in Palm's future chatter hiring SOP. The setup camera reveal is especially low-effort to implement and provides a soft organizational signal. Document acceptable vs. disqualifying desk conditions loosely to avoid over-filtering.

sources.quote: let them hop on a Discord live call with like a shared screen... words per minute test, how fast they can type
ask them, 'Hey, quickly turn your camera around and show me your setup.' Because it shows you so much about what type of person they are.

findings.title: 90-min Loom training video with embedded accountability checkpoints to filter non-completers
findings.claim: Embedding hidden checkpoints (emoji signals, mid-video questions) inside a mandatory 90-minute onboarding video filters out candidates who won't follow through — before they ever touch a live fan account.
findings.what_they_do: After passing all screening stages, selected candidates receive a 90+ minute video covering: how OnlyFans works, how the CRM (Creator Hero) works, scripts, hotkeys, and job structure. The video includes 2–3 embedded accountability checks — e.g., 'send me a cherry emoji if you watched to here' or answer specific questions after viewing. Anyone who fails to complete or respond is cut. Rationale: if a chatter won't watch a Loom on Day 1, they won't perform reliably in month 2.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatter onboarding (§8). This tactic is directly relevant to the AI chatting roadmap project and in-house chat team buildout. Palm already uses Loom-style documentation in other workflows — this pattern is familiar.
findings.recommendation: Design Palm's future chatter onboarding as a video-first, checkpoint-gated flow. Use a single 60–90 min Loom (or similar) covering OF mechanics, Palm's CRM/tools, scripts, and KPIs, with 2–3 embedded compliance signals. Treat failure to complete as an automatic disqualification.

sources.quote: send them like a 90-minute plus video explaining the entire job... include a couple of accountability checks
if someone isn't even watching a Loom video you sent them at their first day on the job, you can imagine how he's going to work in a month from now

findings.title: Supervised shadow → monitored practice → independent KPI ramp for new chatters
findings.claim: New chatters should follow a structured three-phase ramp: shadow existing chatters, then practice under live supervision, then work independently under KPI monitoring — rather than going live solo immediately.
findings.what_they_do: Phase 1 (first ~week): new chatter shadows and observes existing chatters in real shifts. Phase 2: chatter handles 2–3 hours of live chat themselves while a supervisor watches in real time. Phase 3: chatter works independently while a supervisor continues to monitor shifts, gives feedback, and answers questions. KPIs and quality controls are only introduced once the chatter reaches a stable independent baseline.
findings.vs_us: No internal ramp-up process (§8 outsourced). This structured ramp is essential for Palm's in-house chat team plan and maps well to Palm's existing supervisor/manager role structure (§10 chat_manager Clerk role exists).
findings.recommendation: Adopt this three-phase ramp as the standard onboarding track for all in-house chatters. Define the Phase 2→3 promotion trigger (e.g., supervisor scores 3 consecutive satisfactory shifts). Assign the chat_manager Clerk role as the supervisor for Phase 1–2 monitoring. Codify KPI thresholds before Phase 3 begins.

sources.quote: let them watch other people, let them supervise other chatters for a couple of days for the first week. Then let them do their initial practice, let them chat for like two or three hours themselves with a supervisor watching.

findings.title: Hire for coachability and reliability, not pre-existing chatting skill
findings.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
findings.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring criteria (§8 outsourced). This philosophy should anchor Palm's future hiring rubric — it means Palm can source from a much wider talent pool and doesn't need to pay a premium for 'experienced' chatters.
findings.recommendation: Document 'coachability, reliability, honesty' as Palm's three core chatter hiring criteria. Build the scoring rubric for each screening stage around these three traits rather than chatting knowledge. This also reduces cost — experienced chatters command higher pay; trainable reliable people do not.

sources.quote: It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.
